title: What an American thinks about Guyana

transcript.text: What an American thinks about Guyana.
Okay, Guyana is that country on the northern edge of South America.
Guyana, it's a bunch of black people speaking like Jamaicans.
Only they're in English, only they're in South America.
Kind of trippy.
It's a Caribbean country, you know, right there in South America.
They are really, really poor, though.
To get into the city, the country, right there in the border,
It's a river that you could walk across, but there's no bridge.
And I met a guy on the bus, and he wanted me to pay for him to get across.
It was like a dollar.
He paid me on the other end, but then he said he couldn't pay me on the other end,
And then he said his money was stolen,
And he needed me to pay him to get to Georgetown, the capital.
And I shouldn't have gone for it, but I did.
And, yeah, when we got there, oh, whoops, he couldn't pay me.
So I got scammed, but I was hanging out with his friends, he and his friends,
And he was staying like one of his friends with a scam artist.
That old guy, he says he uses his bad eye to make money on the street.
But one of his friends lived in this house that has, like, dirt floor and lived in a hammock.
It was just this, like, house shell right on the edge of town.
It was this town.
It was like the Wild West.
It had dirt road, no paved, but it was on a grid system.
And people would watch their movies on, like, at the bar.
They'd have the movie up there, and then people would go and watch it.
And the guys would talk to the TV.
Like, yeah, man, that Jackie-chan, he like, or that Jet Li, he like Jackie-chan.
He's like, wah-pow, wah-pow, wah-pow, yeah.
And they're, like, talking the whole time of a thing.
And, uh, but, but, yeah, the whole northern, southern part of this country is, like, um, Savannah.
Just big, open grassland.
Not how you would think the jungle would be.
I guess the extreme south was the jungle.
But then the part where you entered from Brazil was, like, Savannah grassland.
And, um, and the road across the country is just dirt road.
Like, four-wheel drive.
Um, they have these big things called Bedford trucks, and the wheel is, like, six feet high.
And they, they, so they dig these big holes in this mud.
So that normal, uh, you know, four-runners can't even drive it.
They get stuck.
They have to be on the side.
And, yeah, dude, I saw a jaguar walking across there through the jungle.
On top of the Bedford.
You had to take, we had to cross one river, and it was, um,
It was, um, we had to, there was no bridge.
You had to have the Bedford get on this thing and carry it across.
And it was dirt road the whole time, but then it became, like, a normal dirt road, not, like, four-wheel drive.
And the, the, the central, the capital city is, uh, really poor.
All of these old, old, old buildings, like, from, like, 150 years ago.
English architecture.
But, man.
Got built once, and now they're just in the state of decay and haven't been painted since they were built.
So, almost all the paint is off.
There were, um, churches all over the place.
Lots of churches.
And lots of little mini, mini cars.
Mini vans where people would drive their cars around.
And, um, you know, instead of big buses, it would be the little mini vans that you'd get on this one and go.
And they were all painted.
And had, like, names on them.
And had, like, music playing in them.
And it was, like, his own car.
And then you'd jump in there.
And he's playing his own music and his own, like, yeah, personalized cars.
But I walked around some.
And one dude was scowling at me.
Like, he wanted to kick my ass.
He was sitting in front of his car.
And so I was like, hey, man, what you doing?
I didn't, it's not a very, I heard it's a dangerous place.
And it's not very touristy.
I was the only tourist I saw there.
Until I saw these other guys on this one bus ride I was in.
And they were these, like, college kids.
And I hung out with them one night.
But, uh, those were the only other ones I saw.
So, just a bunch of black dudes.
I met one white guy who said he was a Guyanese.
And he owned a, uh, he owned a, um, gold mine.
Or a diamond mine in the southern part.
And he was going to fly there.
Or I guess he normally flies.
But this time he was going to, like, take a bus.
And he was going to go get a joint.
But he never came back.
I just did, I was driving, um, when we drove back on the bus.
Back to the Brazil.
We had to cross some checkpoint for some reason.
And the dude didn't want to let us pass.
And he had this big, big rifle.
And he was wasted off his ass.
And he was really pissed off at the car driver.
And, like, wanted to refuse to let everybody go.
And kept accusing the car driver.
That we were all going by of, like, being a dick.
And, like, copping an attitude with him.
And, like, the guy was totally cool.
He was just, like, not saying anything the whole time.
And he's, like, he's just mumbling.
And after, like, a half hour of that, he finally,
He finally decided to stamp us through.
And he was like, okay, get out of here.
And I was in, uh, I also wanted to get,
I had to get stamped in.
When I first went there.
When I was leaving, I had to get stamped out.
So I went, but it wasn't an office.
It was just some guy's house.
And so I went to a guy's house.
And I was standing there, like, in front of the front door.
And I was like, hey, I just need to get stamped.
And he kind of, like, was like, oh, yeah.
Or something.
And then I was just, like, standing there for, like, 10 minutes or 20 minutes.
Long time while I was just watching him, like, walk around.
And then he stops and he goes, hey, I'm not on duty right now.
And I was like, oh, well, when should I come back?
And he's like, oh, here, I'll just do it.
That was because it was.
But the guys driving me back, they bought me a beer.
And some, like, town, some, like, house in the middle of nowhere.
There's nothing between the southern edge, this little town where I went in, across the border, and then Georgetown.
There's, like, nothing.
It's just uninhabited.
There's one lady.
I guess she just manned a house where people would get food in the middle of the jungle.
And she was, uh, I got some food there, like soup.
But she was the only house.
And there was some guy who, I guess, worked for her.
I don't know where he lived.
He had, like, two fingers.
And he didn't seem to be, like, able to talk or something.
But she was, like, angry with him.
This big afro dude.
She's like, hey, man, you don't, you're not going to let you do that or something.
And he, like, walked off.
And then a couple miles later, I saw the jaguar.
So I guess jaguars don't attack people.
I read in the newspaper that a jaguar, somebody found a jaguar hanging out on his front porch.
But then he shot it dead.
I was talking to one guy.
He was a, uh, he imported, um, he was exporting, uh, rare birds.
Like, endangered birds.
And he went into the jungle and would get them.
And he said he saw some.
He said the Indians out there, they, uh, they can hypnotize.
Like, he said the big snakes out there are huge anacondas.
And they can hypnotize deer.
But he said he saw some huge monster thing while he was out in a, but the Indians out there, they have these bones.
And because they have the, uh, the snake tooth necklace, the snake sees the necklace and knows it's a snake tooth.
And so then they're afraid of the, uh, the Indian.
And they don't bother the Indian.
But, um, um, yeah, there's a couple of, they're, they speak this Creole, this weird Creole that you can barely understand.
But you can't understand at all if they talk to each other.
But they write in the Creole, too.
I read a newspaper and they were, it was like their Creole writing right there in the newspaper.
It was really weird.
I could understand it, but, like, barely.
And, uh, yeah.
And there's some Indians there, too, from India.
Um, who I guess contribute to the language.
So, this is what an American thinks about Guyana.
